questions.question: What is the normal, inherent rate for the AV junction?
questions.explanation: The AV junction's own natural pacemaker rate is 40-60 bpm — slower than the SA node's 60-100 bpm, which is why the junction normally stays quiet unless the SA node fails to fire.

questions.question: Which of the following dysrhythmias is NOT considered part of the supraventricular tachycardia classification?
questions.explanation: Supraventricular means 'above the ventricles' — so any tachycardia originating in the atria, SA node, or AV junction qualifies. Ventricular tachycardia, by definition, originates below that point, in the ventricles, so it's excluded from the SVT category entirely.

questions.question: What sign or symptom might a patient complain about when experiencing a supraventricular tachycardia in an unstable condition?
questions.explanation: An unstable SVT means the fast rate is compromising the patient's circulation — chest pain and discomfort is a red flag sign that the heart isn't perfusing adequately at that rate, distinct from the milder fluttering sensation of palpitations alone.

questions.question: The criterion needed to classify a dysrhythmia as a supraventricular tachycardia is:
options: a heart rate between 150 and 350 beats per minute.
a clear, easily identifiable P wave with the entire wave visualized.
atrial and ventricular rates that are not the same.
questions.explanation: SVT is defined largely by rate — 150 to 350 bpm — precisely because the P wave is often hard to identify clearly at these speeds, which is part of what makes pinpointing the exact origin so tricky.

questions.question: What is the primary difficulty in determining a supraventricular rhythm?
options: Determining the origin of the tachycardia
questions.explanation: At very fast atrial rates, the P wave often gets buried or distorted, making it genuinely difficult to tell whether the rhythm originated in the atria, the SA node, or the AV junction — even though the rate and QRS width might be easy enough to measure.

questions.question: When is the identification of the specific dysrhythmia important in terms of treatment of the patient?
options: When the patient first complains of any signs or symptoms
When the patient's heart rate has decreased to a rate of 100 beats per minute
During the treatment of a fast tachycardia situation
After the rhythm has been converted to a normal rhythm and/or the heart rate is between 60 and 100 beats per minute
questions.explanation: In an acute unstable tachycardia, treatment needs to happen fast — figuring out the exact SVT subtype in the moment matters because it can change the specific intervention chosen, even before every detail of the rhythm's origin is fully sorted out.

questions.question: What is the heart rate range for junctional escape rhythm?
questions.explanation: Junctional escape rhythm runs at the AV junction's own natural rate of 40-60 bpm — this is what takes over as a backup pacemaker when the SA node fails to fire.

questions.question: What is the heart rate range for accelerated junctional rhythm?
questions.explanation: Accelerated junctional rhythm runs faster than the junction's normal 40-60 bpm rate, but still within the normal overall range of 60-100 bpm — hence 'accelerated' rather than 'tachycardia.'

questions.question: What is the heart rate range for junctional tachycardia?
questions.explanation: Junctional tachycardia pushes past the normal range entirely, into 100-150 bpm — the fastest of the three junctional rhythm variants (escape, accelerated, and tachycardia).
